Information on the amount of the service fee of the service provider can be found here The following visa fees are charged: Schengen visas (residence of less than 90 days): Children up to six years old Free of charge Children between 6 and 12 years old 45 euros Applicants from 12 years of age 90 euros National visas (residence of more than 90 days): Children between 0 and 18 years old 40 euros Applicants from 18 years of age 75 euros The following persons are exempt from the visa fee: spouses, registered same-sex partners and underage unmarried children of German nationals Parents of minor Germans family members of: EU -/ EEA -nationals, provided they enjoy freedom of movement. Foreigners who receive a scholarship from public funds for their long-term stay in Germany, as well as their spouses or partners and minor children, insofar as they are included in the support. Researchers within the meaning of Article 3(2) of the Directive: EU ) 2016/801 for travel for research purposes or for participation in a scientific seminar or conference. Foreign exhibitors at German trade fairs who present an official trade fair certificate. The family reunification of a child to the parents is only possible if the parental relationship between the parent and the child is unambiguously proven. With regard to surrogacy, German law is much more restrictive than the law of origin of other states. German ancestry law basically ascribes motherhood to the child to the conferring surrogate mother. The reunification of the child born by surrogate mother to Germany is therefore not possible in principle. In order to validate the parentage of the child from the adopting parents for the German legal area, additional procedural steps such as obtaining DNA reports and custody decisions from Iranian courts are usually necessary. Bear in mind, therefore, that the length of the procedure for a visa application, in which a parentage relationship created by surrogacy is decisive, can be extremely lengthy. title: Afghan nationals: permanent residence or Family reunification
text: Afghan nationals: permanent residence or Family reunification 27.03.2022 - Artikel +++Important note for persons with Afghan citizenship: Registration in this appointment category is only permitted if you have already continuously registered at the time of your personal interview. Have been in Iran for at least six months . Afghan applicants residing in Afghanistan must register via the appointment system of the Embassy Kabul (Tehran can be chosen as the place of application). Further information is available on the following website: Message Kabul: Long-term stay - over 90 days +++ Please sort all your application documents in the order given below in two complete and separate sentences! All copies must be submitted in Din A4 format. This also applies if the original has a different format. All documents must be submitted exclusively in German or in German translation. In the embassy building, there is no way to copy or print documents! Please submit the following documents in this order at your appointment at the counter: Signed by the applicant Application form for permanent residence/long-term visas and instruction acc. Section 54 AufenthG . The table for ‘Additional accessibility and representation’ shall be completed and signed by applicants. Passport photo in black and white or colour, size (35x45 mm), not older than 6 months, white or grey background, biometrical. Please note the photo sample board and explanations (only in German). valid, machine-readable passport with signature, as well as two copies in Din A4 format of pages 2 and 3 of your passport Proof of your habitual residence in Iran (at least six months) e.g. by Iranian residence permit or refugee card Copy of the identity card or residence status of your spouse/parent/child in Germany (original not required) Important note: In the case of family reunification to the recognized refugee, the BAMF Notification of the reference person in Germany at the time of application. Marriage certificate (Nikah Nameh): For the first and second sentence, simple copies with translation are sufficient. However, the original of the marriage certificate must be presented when applying. Important note: If your marriage was concluded in proxy, you must also file a proxy power of attorney. This must be dated before marriage and have the corresponding extent (spouse, morning gift, etc.) as well as be certified by a notary or an Afghan representative. If you have a religious marriage certificate, please submit it along with the translation. Ikrar Nameh: The document confirms the registration of the Afghan marriage in the central registers. Responsible for the exhibition are the Supreme Court in Kabul and its branches in the provinces. (An Afghan marriage certificate issued in Iran is not enough!) For the first and second sentences, simple copies with translation are sufficient. However, the original must be presented at the time of application. Tazkiras of both spouses/parents/child The German spouse only needs the Tazkira for German-Afghan dual-state nationals. For the first and second sentences, simple copies with translation are sufficient. However, the original must be presented at the time of application. For previous marriages: certificate of divorce/death certificate of the previous spouse For the first and second sentences, simple copies with translation are sufficient. However, the original must be presented at the time of application. Proof of simple knowledge of the German language (level A1) Proof of knowledge of German is only possible through language certificates of the Goethe-Institut e.V. / the German Language Institute Tehran (DSIT), the Austrian Cultural Forum (ÖKF) / the ÖSD Examination Center Shiraz, the TestDaF Institute e.V. and the telc GmbH. In the absence of proof, the application may be rejected. Depending on the residence status of the spouse/parent/child living in Germany and the existence of a timely notification acc. § 29 Abs. 2 AufenthG can be waived from proof of German language skills. In this case, a proof of the time-limited notification in simple copy must be attached to both sentences. Proof of a health insurance valid in Germany (to be submitted in the interview) either valid proof that it applies after entry e.g. card of a German health insurance) or a travel health insurance (validity period: 90/180 days; multiple entry; Scope: Schengen; Cost recovery: min. EUR 30,000. Upon application, the visa fees due shall be paid in euro. The money must be paid in cash and if possible appropriately, otherwise the visa application will not be accepted Info Important: Please note that the presentation of falsified or falsified documents always results in the rejection of your visa application and is punished by the embassy without exception within the framework of the legal requirements existing in Germany. Even if third parties assist you in the preparation of your visa application, you as an applicant are responsible for the accuracy of the information provided in your visa application as well as the authenticity and content of the attached documents. Any necessary sending of the visa application to the foreigners authority will not take place immediately after the application has been submitted. The entire processing including the participation of the foreigners authority usually takes four to six months from the date of application. However, it may also take longer. Once your visa application is decided, you will receive a telephone message. Requests for progress will be answered only after six months from the date of the request. title: Certificates
text: Certificates Photo of a stamp © colourbox Artikel The embassy can issue the following certificates for you: Certificates of life Closure of a blocked account Export certificate Removal certificate Consular certificate to apply for a visa at foreign embassies in Tehran Other consular certificates Certificates of life If you receive your pension abroad, you must prove annually that the conditions for receiving the payments are still met. You can have the confirmation made by personal consultation with a valid identity document directly at the embassy. You must then send the documents yourself to the pension fund. The certificate shall be free of charge. Closure of a blocked account If you want to cancel your already set up blocked account because your visa application has been rejected, this is usually possible by submitting your refusal decision. If you want to cancel your already set up blocked account because you will not be studying in Germany and have not applied for a visa, you need a fee-bearing consular certificate. The background is that blocked accounts cannot be closed without official proof. For a speech at the German Embassy in Tehran, you need a Date of application for a consular certificate and your passport. In addition, the Fee for consular certificate with presentation an. If you have already entered Germany, please contact your competent foreigners authority, which must agree to a possible dissolution. The German Embassy in Tehran is not responsible in this case. Export certificate All information can be found in our Information leaflet . Removal certificate If you want to move out of Iran and bring moving goods to Germany, you may need a moving certificate. The German Embassy in Tehran can issue you a one-time removal certificate. The following documents are required: Fully completed and signed Application form Passport (original and copy), Iranian residence or work permit (original and copy) showing the period of stay in Iran (at least 1 year) to be certified. The processing time is 2 - 3 days. Information about the applicable fee can be found here . Further information on this topic can be found here . Consular certificate to apply for a visa at foreign embassies in Tehran The most common case is consular certificates for visa applications at other foreign embassies in Tehran. In the visa application certificate for foreign embassies in Tehran, the German Embassy confirms that the applicant is in possession of a German passport. The decision whether the visa is finally granted lies exclusively with the responsible foreign embassy. Only German citizens with a habitual residence in Iran or without a current habitual residence (e.g. world travelers) can obtain such a certificate from the embassy. As proof of habitual residence in Iran, a deregistration certificate from Germany and an Iranian residence permit (for dual nationals: Iranian passport) must be presented. Please inquire in advance at the respective foreign embassy whether such a certificate is required there. Attention: The Embassy of Tehran does not issue consular certificates for countries for which the Federal Foreign Office has issued a travel warning. Other consular certificates The presentation of other consular certificates can be required by both German and Iranian authorities in administrative procedures to officially confirm certain facts. Typical examples include: Confirmation of a person’s habitual residence in Iran for submission to German courts or tax offices Confirmation of possession of a German passport for presentation to Iranian authorities Information about the applicable fee can be found here . An appointment is required to apply for consular certificates that are not related to applying for a visa at foreign embassies or pension matters. Please book your appointment Online . title: Certificates and attestations
text: Certificates and attestations Siegel Notar © www.colourbox.com Artikel Certifications Certificates Online proceedings of notaries in Germany in company law Please be sure to read this Information leaflet There are basically two different forms of drawing up public documents in German law: the form of signature certification and the form of notarial deed. In both forms, a document is signed. Legislators and case law determine when which form applies. The foreign notary or the foreign authority can not always help when it comes to marriage contracts, declarations in child matters, wills, requests for inheritance certificates or land purchase contracts, which are needed in Germany. Often the German embassies and consulates are the right contacts. Certifications Certification of copies or photographs (copy certification) Copy certificates must be requested through the external service provider, more information can be found here . Signature certification For this purpose, please bring the document on which your signature is to be certified and your passport or identity card. Typical signature certifications are for example: Application for a certificate Declaration of accession/authorisation to declare marriage (With this declaration you authorize your fiancé to register your planned marriage with the registry office in Germany.) You will receive the necessary form from the registry office in Germany responsible for your marriage. Authorisations Power of attorney for the establishment of a GmbH Declarations of approval of sales contracts concluded in Germany () In addition to your passport and the declaration, please bring the purchase contract already certified in Germany. Powers of attorney to conclude a purchase contract require notarization in certain individual cases. This is the case, among other things, if the power of attorney is irrevocably granted. Please discuss this problem in advance with your lawyer or notary in Germany .) Decree of inheritance certificates of life; Information about the applicable fee can be found here . Here You can book an appointment for signature certification. In the case of the following transactions, the signature cannot generally be certified: Opening of accounts Under the Money Laundering Act, German missions abroad are not authorised to carry out signature certifications and identity checks for account openings, lending and similar cases. This also includes credit card applications. Signature certifications must be rejected. Please contact the bank for further action, which has sent you the documents or referred you to the German mission abroad for identity verification. A special rule applies only to blocked accounts of “visa-required” foreign students. Here, the missions abroad can continue to carry out signature certifications and identity checks if the opening of a blocked account - taking into account the local circumstances - is the most reliable way for the student to prove his financial situation. General power of attorney (please contact a notary in Germany) Affirmative insurance (these must be certified). Please refer to the information in the following section. Certificates In the case of some legal transactions, legal validity requires a notarisation () e.g. Recognition of paternity, applications for inheritance certificates, general powers of attorney, certain real estate transactions. Please contact the consulate in advance to clarify the details. An affidavit shall be made: e.g. required to confirm the marital status of the Iranian fiancée. In such a case, please contact the consulate by mail in advance for the purpose of appointment. In order to submit a solemn insurance on marital status, the following documents must be submitted for the presentation: Valid passport of the person providing the affidavit Proof from the German registry office/the German authority that it requests the presentation of the affidavit (in German) e.g. E-mail correspondence of the registry office with the German fiancé, leaflet of the competent registry office If the Iranian fiancée has already been married: additional proof of prior marriage and dissolution / divorce Fee Online proceedings of notaries in Germany in company law In the area of company law, it is also possible to contact a German notary directly through a Online procedure To carry out notarizations and certifications also from abroad. All information on this page is based on findings and assessments of the message at the time of writing. However, no guarantee can be given for the completeness and correctness, in particular because of changes that have occurred in the meantime. title: Change of residence
text: Change of residence Passport with various entry stamps © dpa Artikel General instructions An appointment is necessary to apply for new German passports and ID cards. Attention: The list of documents to be submitted is not exhaustive. In individual cases, further evidence may be required. How do I apply for the change of residence? Read the information carefully. A personal interview is not necessary. The application for the change of residence can also be made by an authorised representative. Put together the necessary documents. Complete the application form in full. Make an appointment about it. Appointment system The German Embassy in Tehran Bring all documents in the original with a simple (not certified) copy. incomplete applications cannot be received; You must then book a new appointment and audition again with complete documents. What documents do I need? Application on change of residence signed by yourself (for minors: signature of all custodians) If you do not speak for the change of residence: Power of attorney, signed by you (for minors: signature of all custodians) Passport and identity card Attention: If you have several valid German passports, they must all be presented for a change of residence. Deregistration of the last residence: * always required if the last residence was in Germany *may be required if you lived in another country where there was a reporting system () e.g. Switzerland Proof of the permanent transfer of your center of life to Iran, e.g. Rental contract, employment contract, water bill Iranian residence permit or passport A change of residence can only be requested by prior appointment. You can make an appointment about it. Appointment system booking. All information on this page is based on findings and assessments of the message at the time of writing. However, no guarantee can be given for the completeness and correctness, in particular because of changes that have occurred in the meantime. title: Customs matters
text: Customs matters German customs insignia © Bundeszollverwaltung Artikel Notification requirement for cash in EU - Travel services Since 15. Regulation (EC) No 1889/2005 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 26 June 2007 applies in all Member States. October 2005 (OJ 2005.) EU L 309 of 25 November 2005, page 9 on the control of cash entering or leaving the European Union. Travellers must be at Entry into the EU or leave from the EU pursuant to Article 3(1) of Regulation (EC) No 1889/2005 iVm Section 12a(1) iVm Section 1(4) Customs Administration Act (ZollVG) cash in the amount of 10,000 euros or more or, in the case of other currencies (e.g. Swiss francs, US dollars, British pound), declare the corresponding equivalents in writing to the competent national authority, usually the customs authority, using the form 0400 or 0401 on its own initiative. This is not to be confused with an authorisation for the import and export of cash, which is still not required. Travellers who, using equivalent means of payment, have a total value of 10,000 euros or more from a non-European EU -State to Germany or from Germany to a non- EU If you leave the country, you must notify this amount verbally to the customs officer upon questioning. In the case of a check, there is an obligation to provide the control officers with information on their origin, the beneficial owner and the intended use; and If applicable, submit documentation. The term cash is defined in application forms 0400 and 0401 (German and English, page 2). The registration forms (in German and English), leaflets and further information can be found on the website of the German customs . Also in Travel within the EU are cash carried as well as equivalent means of payment from 10,000 euros (or equivalent in other currency) according to § 12a paragraph 2 iVm § 1 paragraph 4 ZollVG on questions to the customs officer verbally to report, as well as their origin, the beneficial owner and the purpose of these cash and equivalent means of payment. Additional information can be found on the Website of the European Commission be seen. For further questions, travelers can contact the General Customs Directorate - Central Information PO Box 100761 01077 Dresden Internet: www.zoll.de Tel. 0351 44834-510 Email: info.privat@zoll.de fax: 0351 44834-590 Removal certificate Answers to all questions concerning the importation of goods to Germany can be found on the homepage of the customs authority under www.zoll.de. Information on duty exemption in connection with a move to Germany can be found there if you enter the keyword “movement goods” as a search term. You can send specific questions directly to the customs authority: info@zoll-infocenter.de Here you will find the Removal certificate application form . Import and export of animals and plants of endangered species Many animal and plant species are now endangered or even threatened with extinction worldwide as a result of trade interests in their stock. In order to effectively address this threat, the Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna a Flora (CITES) was concluded in 1973. Further information on Species protection Import of copied branded products In the case of counterfeit or counterfeit goods without a commercial character, which you transport in your personal luggage as a tourist on a trip to Germany, the customs authority does not intervene in accordance with the regulations of industrial legal protection. However, if the nature and quantity of the counterfeit or counterfeit goods, your person or other circumstances give rise to indications of commercial activity, this exception cannot be used. The counterfeit or counterfeit goods shall not be imported for commercial purposes. A commodity is used in the course of trade if it serves any business purpose of its own or that of another person. This applies irrespective of whether the quantitative and value limits applicable to the relief from import duty have been respected. Baggage forwarded or forwarded on the same transport route is subject to the same import regulations as your personal baggage. However, if your luggage is forwarded or forwarded by post or courier service, it is not considered to be carried and the rules for postal items apply. Further information on: Counterfeiting All information on this page is based on findings and assessments of the message at the time of writing. However, no guarantee can be given for the completeness and correctness, in particular because of changes that have occurred in the meantime. title: Data protection declaration in accordance with art. 13 GDPR
text: Data protection declaration in accordance with art. 13 GDPR 10.11.2023 - Artikel The following information serves to inform you when we collect what data on this website and how we use it. 1. Basic principles 2. Data processing in connection with visiting this website 3. Processing of personal data in the context of contact 4. Newsletter delivery 5. Ordering of printed matter 6. Events, press and public relations 7. Your rights 8. Data protection contact person of the mission abroad The Federal Foreign Office and the German missions abroad process personal data in accordance with the European General Data Protection Regulation (European Data Protection Regulation) GDPR ) , Federal Data Protection Act (Bundesdatenschutzgesetz) BDSG ) and, where appropriate, special legal provisions. To inform you about data processing and your rights and our obligation to provide information (Art. 13 and 14) GDPR ), we inform you below about the processing of personal data when using our website, when contacting (via the contact form, by e-mail, by phone or by post) and when subscribing to one of our newsletters. 1. Basic principles 1.1. Controller and Data Protection Officer Pursuant to Section 2 of the Act on the External Action Service (GAD), the Federal Foreign Office (headquarters) and the missions abroad together form a single federal authority. Responsible for the processing of personal data gem. Article 4(7) GDPR is: Federal Foreign Office Werderscher Markt 1 10117 Berlin Germany / Germany Telephone: +49 (0)30 18 17-0 / Citizen service: +49 (0)30 18 17-2000 Fax: +49 (0)30 18 17-3402 Contact form Citizen Service You can contact the Federal Foreign Office Data Protection Commissioner at: Federal Foreign Office Data Protection Commissioner Federal Foreign Office Werderscher Markt 1 10117 Berlin Germany / Germany Telephone: +49 (0)30 18 17-7099 Fax: +49 (0)30 18 17-5 7099 Contact form Data Protection Officer If you have any questions about data protection, you can also contact the data protection contact persons of the missions abroad. You can find out how to reach the data protection contact person of the mission abroad at the bottom of the page (see point 8). 1.2. Personal data Personal data is any information relating to an identified or identifiable natural person. An identifiable person is considered to be a natural person who can be identified directly or indirectly – in particular by assignment to an identifier such as a name, an identification number, location data, an online identifier (Art. 4 No. 1). 1 GDPR ) 1.3. Legal basis of processing We process your personal data on the basis of Art. 6 para 1 lit. a GDPR if you have consented to the processing () e.g. newsletter delivery, Art. 6 para 1 lit. b GDPR , insofar as the processing is necessary for the performance of a contract or pre-contractual measures, Art. 6 para 1 lit. c) GDPR to the extent that the processing is necessary to fulfil a legal obligation incumbent on us, Article 6(3) 1 lit. dd GDPR where the vital interests of the data subject or another natural person require the processing of personal data; Art. 6 para 1 lit. e GDPR iVm Section 3 BDSG , to the extent that the processing is necessary for the performance of a task entrusted to us in the public interest or in the exercise of official authority e.g. Answering contact requests. The public tasks of the Federal Foreign Office and the German missions abroad include, in particular, public relations work and the provision of information about the Federal Republic of Germany abroad. § 1 of the Act on the External Action Service – GAD ) 2. Data processing in connection with visiting this website 2.1. Data collection Every time a user accesses our Internet offer and every time a file is retrieved, data about this process is temporarily stored and processed in a log file. The tracking tool matomo used for usage analyses is configured so that no personal data of the visitor is collected. Furthermore, your IP address is anonymized so that visitors cannot be tracked. The tracking tool is available on own servers in a secure, BSI -certified network zone, so that no tracking data reaches the outside. In addition, you can adjust the settings in your browser so that your visit to the website is not registered and evaluated at all (do-not-track setting). By transmitting a browser variable, the tracking tool is signaled whether visitors want to be recorded by the tracking tool or not. In order to know how to do this with your browser, give e.g. into a search engine: "Do not track activate [name of your browser]" The following data is collected via JavaScript tracking: Request (file name of requested file), date and time of request Browser type/version (e.g. Firefox 115.0) Browser language (e.g. German) Operating system used (e.g. Windows 11) Device type, device model, device brand Screen resolution Plugins used: () e.g. Flash, Java Referrer URL (the previously visited page) Anonymised IP address Country and region from which the request was made Downloads Clicks Number of forms sent (depending on destination definitions) 2.2. Session Cookies When accessing individual pages, so-called temporary cookies are used to facilitate navigation. These session cookies do not contain any personal data and expire after the session has expired. Cookies are also used when using the shopping cart. These also expire at the end of the sitting. Technologies that make it possible to understand the access behavior of users are not used. 2.3. Use of Social media plug-ins We are currently using the following Social media plug-ins one: Facebook, X, Instagram and YouTube (Google). We use them to do so. so-called Two-click solution. This means that if you visit our site, initially no personal data will be transferred to the providers of the Plug-ins forwarded. Only when you click on the activation button of the social networks will personal data be transferred from you to the respective Plug-in Suppliers and there (for US providers in the USA ) stored. We use them. Social media plug-ins , to make our information offer more interesting by interacting with you and other users. We expressly draw your attention to the fact that the social networks store the data of their users. e.g. store personal information, IP address, etc. in accordance with their data usage policies and use them for business purposes. You can find out about the processing of data by social networks under the following links: Facebook , X , Instagram , YouTube (Google) We have no influence on the data collection and its further use by social networks. To what extent, at what location and for what duration the data is stored, to what extent the networks comply with existing erasure obligations, which evaluations and links with the data are carried out and to whom the data is passed on, is not visible to us. We therefore ask you to check exactly which personal data you make available to social networks via the use of our site. If you do not want the social networks to receive personal data through your communication via our site, you should contact us in other ways, for example via the contact form on this website. 2.4 Google Maps We bind on our website the map service “ Google Maps “ of the provider” Google ’ one. When using the service (by clicking on the card), Google Personal data is processed, in particular IP addresses and location data of users, which are not collected without your consent (usually carried out within the settings of their mobile devices). Service provider: Google Ireland Limited, Gordon House, Barrow Street, Dublin 4, Ireland Website: https://maps.google.de Data protection declaration: https://policies.google.com/privacy Opportunity to appeal () Opt-out plugin : http://tools.google.com/dlpage/gaoptout?hl=en 3. Processing of personal data in the context of contact When contacting us, the data you provide (your name, your e-mail address and If applicable, Your address, telephone and fax number) and the submitted content information ( If applicable, personal data) processed for the purpose of contacting and processing your request and in accordance with the time limits applicable to the storage of written material Registration Directive , which are the Common Rules of Procedure of the Federal Ministries (GGO) supplemented, stored. If we are not responsible for your request, the transfer to other state and federal authorities can take place without consent in compliance with data protection if it can be assumed that the transfer is in your interest for the purpose of answering and that no sensitive personal data is affected (Art. 6 paragraph 1 lit.). e GDPR in conjunction with § 3 BDSG § 25 para. 1 in conjunction with § 23 para. 1 No 1 BDSG ) 4. Newsletter delivery You can subscribe to one of our newsletters, with which we inform you about news and press information from the Federal Foreign Office or travel and security advice. For subscription to one of our newsletters we use the so-called Double opt-in procedure. This means that, after your registration, we will send you an e-mail to the specified e-mail address, in which we ask you to confirm that you wish to send the newsletter. Only after confirmation of the link in the e-mail will your e-mail address for newsletter delivery be stored for the duration of use of our newsletter offer. If you do not confirm your registration within 24 hours, your information will be automatically deleted. You can revoke your consent to the sending of the newsletter at any time and unsubscribe from the newsletter. You can revoke it by clicking on the information provided in each newsletter e-mail Link Explanation. 5. Ordering of printed matter If you order brochures, leaflets and other printed products via our website, we require the following personal data to process your order: Surname, first name, street, number, Postcode and location Email address If the order cannot be finally processed by us, the data provided by you will be sent to third parties (shipping company, If applicable, other authorities or institutions, if they ship the ordered material. The legal basis for the processing of your data is your consent in accordance with art. 6 par. Article 1 a GDPR ) If the aforementioned data is not available, the order cannot be processed. The data you provide will be deleted 90 days after the order has been processed. 6. Events, press and public relations As part of their duties, the Federal Foreign Office and the German missions abroad act as organisers or co-organisers of various events. In the event of an invitation to or participation in an event, we process personal data (name, address, etc.). This can be done via various communication channels, for example by filling out a form, a web form, by e-mail, by telephone or by handing over a business card. Which personal data will be transmitted to us in this case results from the respective form, the respective input mask or the data requested in a possible conversation. 6.1. Data collection of accompanying persons For the proper handling of the event, data from accompanying persons of the invited participants are also collected and processed. These, as well as all other data subjects, are entitled to the rights listed in Section 7. 6.2. Data collection from publicly available sources The Federal Foreign Office also collects and processes data from publicly accessible sources, such as homepages of the persons concerned or from publicly accessible directories. 6.3. Further procedure for these data collections The personal data are not transferred to third parties without authorization, but are stored and processed for our internal use and for the purpose of organizing and handling events (e.g. creating guest lists, enabling access controls, etc.). We may arrange for the transfer to one or more processors who use the personal data on our behalf only for internal use attributable to us. Depending on the type of event, we act as co-organizer alongside another cooperation partner. In these cases, the data collected for event purposes will be transmitted to our cooperation partners, who may also use the data exclusively for this purpose of the proper handling of the event. Which cooperation partners these are in individual cases will be communicated separately in the context of the organization of the event. The data will only be stored for as long as is necessary for the organization and handling of the events as well as for our tasks in the area of public relations. As a recipient of cover letters/invitations, you can object to the sending of further cover letters/invitations at any time. 6.4. Image recordings For the purposes of the press and public relations work of the Federal Foreign Office and the German missions abroad, pictures are taken and published at events and appointments, where they can be seen recognizably. You can object to the recording and/or publication. Please contact the photographer directly or use the above-mentioned contact details for your objection. Please note that we have no influence on recordings of press and media representatives. They themselves are responsible in the sense of GDPR . 7. Your rights 7.1. You have the following rights towards us with regard to the personal data concerning you: Right to information, art. 15 GDPR Right to correction, art. 16 GDPR Right to erasure (“right to be forgotten”), art. 17 GDPR Right to restriction of processing, art. 18 GDPR Right to data portability, art. 20 GDPR Right to object to processing, art. 21 GDPR 7.2. As far as the processing of your personal data is based on your consent () e.g. Newsletter), you have the right to revoke it at any time without giving reasons. The revocation of the consent does not affect the legality of the processing carried out on the basis of the consent until the revocation (Art. 7 para). 3 GDPR ) 7.3. You also have the right to complain to a data protection supervisory authority about our processing of your personal data (Art. 77) GDPR ) The supervisory authority responsible for the Federal Foreign Office and the German missions abroad is: The Federal Commissioner for Data Protection and Freedom of Information Graurheindorfer Straße 153 D-53117 Bonn 8. Data protection contact person of the mission abroad You can contact the data protection contact person of the mission abroad at: Data protection contact person Embassy of the Federal Republic of Germany Tehran Avenue Ferdowsi 320-324 PO Box: 11365-179 Tehran, Iran Contact details Sharing page WhatsApp Facebook X Mail upwards leave whitespace for propper svg loading

title: Deaths
text: Deaths Death © www.colourbox.com Artikel Transfer to Germany Burial in Iran Death certificates If a family member or friend dies, this is a cause for grief. When dying abroad, additional organizational questions and problems arise to mourn, which must be solved. In the event of the death of a German citizen, the German Embassy in Tehran can arrange for the survivors to be notified and advise on the completion of the formalities on site and assist in the transfer of the body to Germany. In principle, cremation is not possible in Iran due to Islamic laws. All information can also be found in our Information leaflet in the case of deaths of German nationals in the district of the Embassy in Tehran. Below we have summarized the most important points for you: Transfer to Germany Since a cremation of the corpse cannot be possible in Iran for cultural reasons, there is only the possibility to transfer the corpse in the coffin by air. Unlike in Germany, it is not traditional funeral companies that are responsible for this, but regular logistics companies. The embassy knows the following private companies that offer and carry out transfer to the Federal Republic of Germany. Communication is possible in English. All mentioned service providers operate nationwide. The transfer takes place independently of the place of death usually via Tehran. Aircraft International Ltd 214 Somaye Ave., Tehran 15816 – 63411 Iran Tel: +98 21 8882 78 77 Email: laleh@airlandco.com Website: www.Airlandco.com MARM; Medical Assistance, Roiadside Management Hakim Highway, Sheykh Bahayi St, Tehran Tower, A Block, Tehran, headquarters in Turkey Tel: +98 21 9666 5044 Mobile: +98 912 3477 991 Email: marm@marm.com.tr, farshbaf@marm.com.tr Partner company ADACöglich. The list shall be in alphabetical order. SOS Iran Assistance No 24, 15th St., Gandi Ave, Tehran 15178 Tel: +98 21 8864 8400 Mobile: +98 912 130 1433 Email: ashkan.lahiji@iranassistance.com, behnam.issari@iranassistance.com Website: www.iranassistance.com Partner companies Among others AXA Burial in Iran In principle, German citizens also have the opportunity to be buried at the German cemetery in Tehran – subject to capacity there. In this regard, the Cemetery Committee of the Evangelical Community of German Language in Tehran and the Embassy are happy to answer questions. Evangelical Church of German Language in Iran Kh. Shahid Sheydai 123/1, Gholhak, 19497/15881 Tehran Tel: +98 (21) 22 606 117 Email: post@kirche.ir https://www.kirche.ir/ Death certificates The procurement of the death certificate on site is carried out by the funeral company in Iran. For the issue, the presentation of the passport and birth certificate of the deceased is required. Subsequently, relatives can apply for a German death certificate to be issued by the competent registry office in Germany on presentation of the death certificate issued, translated and legalised at the embassy. The said funeral companies take care of the translation and legalization of the local death certificate. You should finally have the case of death entered in the German civil status register: Here You can find the form for the registration of an infestation abroad in the death register. title: Declaration on accessibility of this website
text: Declaration on accessibility of this website Artikel Which areas are not accessible? Feedback and contact Conciliation procedure The Federal Foreign Office and its missions abroad endeavour to keep their websites in line with national legislation implementing the Directive. EU ) 2016/2102 of the European Parliament and of the Council. This accessibility statement applies to the currently accessible version of this website. It was created on 04.03.2025. Which areas are not accessible? Parts that are not accessible: PDF downloads. We strive to replace non-accessible PDF files with an accessible version as soon as possible. Recorded video content has no alternatives in some cases. We are trying to provide alternatives as soon as possible after publication if they are not already available at publication. Recorded video content does not have audio descriptions in some cases. We strive to provide audio descriptions as soon as possible after publication if they are not already available at publication. Information, structure and relationships are identifiable. The following shortcomings still exist: Search: In the filter area for the date input (selection), the selected data is partially not output in an arrow navigation. Form page: The mandatory field information with (*) is not at the beginning of the form. Content breaks into a single-column layout. The following shortcomings still exist: Search page: On the page, the views of the date pickers are not fully visible. Furthermore, not all filter options are visible in landscape format. Focus order is task appropriate. The following shortcomings still exist: Form page: The focus is not placed on the list of error messages after sending a faulty contact form. Content page: If a user displays the caption, the focus is not set on it. Link purpose is understandable (in context). The following shortcomings still exist: Homepage: On the homepage, the article link, which is displayed directly below the main menu, is displayed with a long caption because it contains the heading, descriptive text and graphic. Name, role and value are identifiable. The following shortcomings still exist: Form: The mandatory fields are programmatically not marked as such. Homepage: In the lower area there is a slider (menu). The current menu entry is clearly visible. But the programmatic award is missing. Feedback and contact Have you noticed any deficiencies in accessibility to content on this website? Do you have any questions about accessibility? Then you can contact us. Please use the provided feedback form at the bottom of the page. You can also tell us Contact by post or telephone. You can submit your request in German sign language here pose. Please note that the sign telephone is in German sign language. Conciliation procedure The Federal Government Commissioner for the Affairs of Persons with Disabilities has a conciliation body in accordance with § 16 BGG. The conciliation body has the task of resolving conflicts between people with disabilities and public bodies of the Federation. You can turn on the arbitration panel if you are not satisfied with the answers from the above contact option. This is not about finding winners or losers. Rather, the aim is to find a solution to a problem together and out of court with the help of the conciliation body. The arbitration procedure shall be free of charge. Nor do they need legal assistance. On the Website of the Mediation Panel You will find all information about the conciliation procedure (in German and English). There you can read how a conciliation procedure works and how you submit the request for conciliation. You can also submit the application in Easy Language or in German Sign Language. You can reach the Conciliation Panel at the following address: Conciliation body according to the Disability Equality Act to the Federal Government Commissioner for the Affairs of Persons with Disabilities Wall Street 53 10117 Berlin Telephone: +49 (0)30 18 527-2805 Fax: +49 (0)30 18 527-742901 E-mail: info@schlichtungsstelle-bgg.de Internet: www.schlichtungsstelle-bgg.de Zurück zu: "Startseite" Sharing page WhatsApp Facebook X Mail upwards leave whitespace for propper svg loading

title: EU Blue Card
text: Blue card EU Artikel Conditions: If you have a recognised university or university degree and already have a concrete job offer with a salary of at least 48,300 EUR (2025) gross per year, you can use the Blue Card EU request. For highly qualified foreigners in the so-called MINT Professions (mathematics, engineering, natural sciences, technology) and doctors a gross annual salary of 43,759.80 EUR (2025) is sufficient for a blue card EU . If you have completed your university or university degree in a country other than Germany, you must have this degree recognised before applying for the visa. Further information can be found under Recognition in Germany and anabine for higher education and university degrees. Please note that there are foreign university degrees that are classified as vocational training in Germany. Allow enough time for the recognition process of your degree and apply for your visa only afterwards! If your salary is less than the required amount, a visa for employment as a professional may come in accordance with § 18 AufenthG under consideration. Documents to be submitted: Please submit all documents in the original with one copy each, as well as in German or with German translation (pre-certified by the Iranian Foreign Ministry). Please submit the documents at your appointment sorted in the order indicated here. Biometric passport photo In colour with white background, size: 35x45mm, not older than 6 months Printing of the application form of VIDEX Including instruction ag. Article 54 AufenthG , signed by hand valid passport, Signed by the passport holder Copy of the second and third passport pages in DIN A4 format for Afghan nationals only: Tazkira, on the basis of which the passport is issued (Paper Azkiras must be pre-certified by the Afghan Ministry of Foreign Affairs). employment contract or Proof of a job offer (from Germany) ) with a gross annual salary of currently at least € 48,300 (as a scientist, mathematician, engineer, doctor or IT specialist: currently at least € 43,760) Declaration on employment , Fully completed by the employer Proof of existing knowledge of German or English Confirmation from the employer of sufficient language skills (if applicable, provide language certificates) If applicable, Description by the employer of how a successful employment without foreign language skills should take place university degree as proof of qualification in the intended field, incl. Overview of grade (Original and copy as well as German translation with pre-certification by the Iranian Foreign Ministry) Proof of recognition of educational qualification in Germany For regulated professions or professions with vocational training: decision of the competent certification body in Germany or Professional Permit/Approbation For non-regulated professions and academic training: 2 printouts from the database ANABINE on comparability of university degrees and Recognition of the university. Please make sure that the name of your university degree and the data set in ANABIN correspond exactly. If college or degree is not covered in ANABIN, you must first Certificate assessment by the Central Office for Foreign Education (ZAB) and submit the ZAB decision. Letter of motivation CVC complete curriculum vitae, in particular with a presentation of the previous training and If applicable, Professional activity Proof of professional activities and history Salary and social security certificates Proof of valid health insurance cover (health insurance valid until employment); Validity from entry for 90 days, vsl. 2 months after visa application visa fee of 75 €, to be paid in euro in cash Further information This information should only give you initial indications and therefore do not claim to be complete. Although they have been prepared with the greatest possible care, liability for the correctness of the content cannot be assumed. From this information alone, no entitlement to the granting of a visa is derived. The applicable residence law is decisive. title: Entrance to another family member
text: Entrance to another family member 09.05.2026 - Artikel Reunification with other family members is regulated in § 36 para. 2 and 3 AufenthG Other family members are family members who are not spouses or minor children. The reunification of other family members is usually not planned and is only Avoidance of exceptional hardness Authorised. This means that the following person or the person in Germany can no longer lead an independent life due to health restrictions, that he is absolutely dependent on the help of the family member, and that this help can only be provided in Germany. The final check whether there is an exceptional hardship in the sense of the law takes place in individual cases after personal consultation and presentation of all necessary documents. Until 31.12.2028, parents and in-laws of foreigners who have resided in Germany as a specialist since 01.03.2024 at the earliest are also able to apply for a visa. Even if not exceptional hardness is present. The granting of such a visa is in principle possible if The foreigner or his spouse on or after 01.03.2024 any of the § 36 Abs. 3 AufenthG has received and continues to hold and have permanent residence in Germany AND the subsistence of the foreigner's parents or in-laws is secured. The table below contains all documents that are normally required for processing the application. Depending on the individual case, further documents may be necessary. In such a case, they shall be invited to submit additional documents in the course of the procedure. Please sort all application documents in the order indicated below. Please submit all copies in the format DIN A4. This also applies if the original has a different format. Foreign-language documents must be translated into the German language and legalized if necessary. The overview below provides information on which documents must be legalized. Information about the legalization process can be found here Biometric passport photo in colour with white background, size: 35x45mm, not older than 6 months. Please note our Photographic sample plate Printing of the application form of VIDEX Including instruction ag. Article 54 AufenthG . The application form must be signed by hand. Valid passport Original + Copy The second and third passport pages The passport should be valid for at least one year upon application. Iranian passports must be signed by the passport holder. Copy of the family member’s passport in Germany If the family member is not German in Germany: Copy of the residence permit Copy the registration certificate of the family member in Germany. At the time of the interview, the registration certificate must not be older than 6 months. Shenasnameh of the following person Original + Original Legalized Translation + Copy Legalized Translation If the relationship does not yet result from this, supplementing: Proof of kinship in the form of civil status documents Iranian documents must Original + Original Legalized Translation + Copy of the Legalized Translation submitted. German certificates must In copy submitted. If available: Court decisions on custody Iranian sentences must Original + Original Legalized Translation + Copy of the Legalized Translation submitted. German judgments must In copy submitted. If it is not a privileged follow-up to a professional: strong evidence of exceptional hardship. These are usually medical findings and diagnoses, as well as a written description of the situation of those affected. The specific documents required depend on the individual case. As part of the procedure, provide any documents that you consider to demonstrate the existence of exceptional hardship. In the course of the procedure, the embassy will request additional documents if necessary. A binding offer of a private health insurance in Germany on the admission of the following person to the health insurance upon entry. The benefits of the health insurance must comply with the framework of § 192 ff. Please pay the visa fee in euro in cash at the time of the hearing at the embassy or the external service provider. The visa fee shall be charged for processing the application and shall not be refunded in the event of refusal of the visa. Applicants who submit their application to the external service provider of the embassy pay the service fee of the service provider in addition to the visa fee. This also applies to applicants who are exempt from the visa fee itself. Information on the amount of the service fee of the service provider can be found here The following visa fees are charged: Schengen visas (residence of less than 90 days): Children up to six years old Free of charge Children between 6 and 12 years old 45 euros Applicants from 12 years of age 90 euros National visas (residence of more than 90 days): Children between 0 and 18 years old 40 euros Applicants from 18 years of age 75 euros The following persons are exempt from the visa fee: spouses, registered same-sex partners and underage unmarried children of German nationals Parents of minor Germans family members of: EU -/ EEA -nationals, provided they enjoy freedom of movement. Foreigners who receive a scholarship from public funds for their long-term stay in Germany, as well as their spouses or partners and minor children, insofar as they are included in the support. Researchers within the meaning of Article 3(2) of the Directive: EU ) 2016/801 for travel for research purposes or for participation in a scientific seminar or conference. Foreign exhibitors at German trade fairs who present an official trade fair certificate. title: Exchange of driving licences
text: Exchange of driving licences 07.08.2022 - Artikel Early exchange of driving licences 1. General Until 19.01.2033, all driving licences issued before 19.01.2013 must be exchanged for new ones. This exchange shall be staggered as follows: Driving licences up to and including 31. were issued in December 1998: Year of birth of the licence holder Date until which the licence must be exchanged Before 1953 19.01.2033 1953-1958 19.01.2022 1959-1964 19.01.2023 1965-1970 19.01.2024 1971 or later 19.01.2025 2. Driving licences issued from 1 January 1999*: Date of issue Date until which the licence must be exchanged 1999-2001 19.01.2026 2002-2004 19.01.2027 2005-2007 19.01.2028 2008 19.01.2029 2009 19.01.2030 2010 19.01.2031 2011 19.01.2032 2012-18.01.2013 19.01.2033 *License holders whose year of birth is before 1953 must exchange the driving licence until 19 January 2033, regardless of the year of issue of the driving licence. After the expiry of the above mentioned period, your old driving license will be invalid. This is only an administrative exchange. Your driving licence will remain unchanged. Additional regular medical examinations or other examinations are not associated with this. They continue to exist only for certain professional groups with special responsibility. The newly issued driving licence is limited to 15 years, regardless of the underlying driving licence. After this period of validity, a new driving licence must be issued. 2. Application procedure for habitual residence in Iran In case of habitual residence in Iran First of all, please contact the driving license authority in Germany, which has issued your current driving license. This sends you the necessary documents, which you filled in and signed back to the German driving licence authority. The German mission abroad is not involved in this process. The application documents are sent to your private address. It is not possible to send the application documents to the German Embassy in Tehran or to return the completed application documents to the German Embassy in Tehran. The cost of the exchange is about 25 EUR plus shipping. The German driving licence authority usually sends the new driving licence to the German Embassy in Tehran for handover to you. Further information about the exchange can be found on the website of the Federal Ministry of Transport and Digital Infrastructure . title: Exit possibilities by land
text: Exit possibilities by land 19.03.2026 - Artikel Iranian airspace is still closed. There is an urgent travel warning from the Federal Foreign Office for Iran. If you are considering leaving by land, please note the following: In a self-responsible assessment of your individual situation, decide whether you are sufficiently safe at your place of residence or current location or want to consider leaving the country due to the tense situation. Please note that the security situation may change at any time. We cannot carry out a situation assessment or risk assessment for your personal circumstances and individual route for you. The opening hours of border crossings can change daily and also at short notice. At all border crossings, longer waiting times must be expected. There are reports that Iranian authorities at the border crossings are refusing Iranian nationals to leave. At the border crossings there is no protection against rocket / drone attacks. Also, it is not excluded that you can get close to military operations when traveling overland. It must be expected when traveling overland with spontaneously set up checkpoints and corresponding time losses. Transport to the border crossings cannot be organised by the Federal Foreign Office. If possible, refrain from night trips and make sure beforehand that you meet the necessary entry requirements of the respective country. If you have a German residence permit but do not have German citizenship, contact the representation of the country of your nationality or the authorities of the neighbouring country to which you wish to enter and inquire about your travel options. Hold back on photo and video footage. In particular, do not take pictures of devices, persons or developments that may be sensitive to safety and do not carry pictures of them that may be stored on the mobile phone. There is a risk of arrests by the Iranian security forces. Be vigilant and follow the news in international and local media. Ensure your communication skills and follow the instructions of the local authorities. The following border crossings are still open according to our information (short-term changes cannot be excluded): Turkey: The border crossings Sero (Esendere) and Bazargan (Gürbulak) are generally open 24 hours. The Kapiköy border crossing is usually open from 8 am to 6 pm (seasonal deviations are possible). The Bazargan (Gürbulak) crossing point should not be used as far as possible for capacity reasons. After the border there are individual possibilities for onward travel. The nearest airports on the Turkish side of the border are in Hakkari, Van and Ağrı. German and Iranian citizens do not need a visa to enter Turkey. Armenia: The Nourdooz crossing is basically open 24 hours. There are direct bus services from Tehran and Tabris to Yerevan and a bus service from the border to the capital Yerevan for the equivalent of about 27,00 EUR / person. German and Iranian citizens do not need a visa to enter the country. Turkmenistan: Due to the complicated entry formalities, departure to Turkmenistan is generally discouraged. The Gaudan-Badjgiran, Lutfabad-Artyk, Inchehborun-Akyayla and Inchehborun-Altyn-Asyr crossing points are open daily from 09:00 to 17:00. The Sarakhs-Sarakhs border crossing is open 24 hours a day. A mandatory requirement for each entry from Iran to Turkmenistan is that the following documents are transmitted to the German Embassy in Ashgabat for each entry person before entering the country: A list with the exact number of people leaving with their names, first names and date of birth. Well-readable colored passport copies of all travelers. Copies of air tickets of all citizens mentioned in the list, including children. Indicate the exact date of arrival and the border inspection point at the Turkmen-Iran border. The exit route with the flights from Turkmenistan. Please note that the maximum permitted stay in Turkmenistan is three days. With this information, the German Embassy in Ashgabat can then send a verbal note to the Turkmen Ministry of Foreign Affairs with a request for assistance in leaving the country. In the case of approval, the invitations required to receive the visa on arrival at the border crossing can then be drawn up on the basis of these documents. The visa fee is $55. Accommodation is provided in the hotels Ashgabat and Sport, according to information from the Ministry of Foreign Affairs of Turkmenistan. In the case of dual nationals of Iranian nationality, the Migration Service shall make an individual decision on the possibility of entering Turkmenistan. Entry cannot be guaranteed. Azerbaijan Entry by land is only possible via the Astara border crossing; all other land borders are generally closed for entry into Azerbaijan for regular passenger transport. For entry, you need a valid German passport, a valid Azerbaijani visa and a special permit from the Azerbaijani authorities. This special permit must be obtained in individual cases through the embassy in Baku. Details about the entry procedure and the application for the special permit can be found on the Website of the German Embassy in Baku . If it is not possible for you to apply for a visa for Azerbaijan, you can also request an exemption visa at the border in Astara when obtaining the special permit. German nationals can then obtain this visa upon entering the border crossing Astara. Buses and taxis are available on the Azerbaijani side of the border for onward travel to the capital Baku. Transportation providers From most major Iranian cities () incl. Tehran and Mashhad have several public and private transport providers offering trips to various border crossings, especially towards Turkey. Bus tickets from Tehran can be purchased at the following locations: South Terminal Bus Station (Tehran, Besat Highway, Khazaneh), telephone: 021-55185422 and 021-55185325 West Terminal Bus Station (Tehran, Azadi roundabout), telephone: 021-44673650 and 021-44669001 East Terminal Bus Station (Tehran, Damavand Str., Serah Tehranpars), telephone: 021-77710909 and 021-77702777 Beyhaghi (Arjantin) Terminal Bus Station (Tehran, Arjantin roundabout), telephone: 021-88534384 and 021-88543426 In addition, bus tickets can be purchased online, e.g. Under: www.safar724.com www.respina24.ir www.mrbilit.com www.sarafmarket.com Zurück zu: "Startseite" Printing page leave whitespace for propper svg loading Sharing page WhatsApp Facebook X Mail upwards leave whitespace for propper svg loading

title: Family law matters
text: Family law matters Father hugs his two sons © Zoonar.com/benis arapovic Artikel Marriages Birth Divorce The German Embassy in Tehran is only responsible for Germans who demonstrably live in Iran. Germans living in Afghanistan should contact the German Embassy in Islamabad. The German Embassy in Tehran is not responsible for Germans in Afghanistan. You need an appointment to speak to the embassy to re-certify a marriage or a foreign birth. Please book one such about our appointment system. Note on the deposited forms: Since 01.05.2025, new regulations in the name law are in force. Please note that in all cases where a name declaration is made under German law, the Choice of Law in German Law must be ticked. Marriages General information about marriages abroad can be found on the Website of the Federal Foreign Office . Registration in the German marriage register Marriages concluded abroad can also be subsequently entered in German marriage registers. For this purpose, use the following Form . Information for German women To the knowledge of the German Embassy Tehran, the German wife is acquired by a Iranian law lawful marriage to an Iranian automatically gives Iranian citizenship. Legally binding information on the acquisition of Iranian citizenship can only be provided by the competent Iranian authorities. German citizenship is not lost in an automatic acquisition of citizenship. In individual cases, the Iranian authorities may at the time of entry or issuance of so-called Shenasnameh for the German wife the German passport of those affected withheld. In this case, you will be asked to contact the German Embassy in Tehran immediately. Please note: for persons who hold both German and Iranian citizenship, the Iranian authorities will treat them exclusively as Iranians. This means that consular care facilities by the German Embassy Tehran are hardly available in emergencies! Declaration of name in marriage or after a divorce Upon marriage, the name of the spouses does not change under Iranian law. In German law, there is the possibility of changing the name after the marriage, the divorce or the death of the spouse. However, you should think carefully about this, as there are problems in Iran. Inquiries from the authorities can be made if the entry in the German passport does not correspond to the entry in another identity document (for example, the Iranian passport). Declaration of names for determining the marriage name Note that both spouses must audition in person to make the statement determining the marriage name. Please also note that Iranian civil status documents in Legalized form must be present. Information about the applicable fee can be found here . Please bring the following documents in the original with two copies each: Fully completed application form for Determination of a Marriage Name Proof of identity (Iranian or German passport/Shenasnameh) Proof of citizenship (passport, certificate of naturalisation, Shenasnameh) Birth certificates of both spouses Marriage certificate If previous marriages existed: divorce judgment or Death certificate of the previous spouse Declaration of name to determine the name after divorce or death of the spouse Please bring the following documents in the original with two copies each. Please note that Iranian civil status documents must be in Legalized form must be present. Information about the applicable fees can be found here . Fully completed application form ‘ Unilateral Declaration on the Marriage Name ‘ Proof of identity (Iranian or German passport/Shenasnameh) Proof of citizenship (passport, certificate of naturalisation, Shenasnameh) Birth certificate Divorce judgment or Death certificate of the spouse Confirmation of the Recognition of divorce German legal field Birth If your child was born in Iran and needs a German passport for the very first time, the name of the child must first be clarified. The name declaration form for a minor child can be found here . However, a name declaration can also be made within the framework of Application for a foreign birth certificate are submitted. Note that both when declaring the name and when applying for a foreign birth certificate, both parents must audition in person together with the child. Iranian civil status documents must be in Legalized form present. Information about the applicable fees can be found here . Please bring the following documents in the original with two copies each: Fully completed form (see above) Proof of identity (Iranian or German passport/Shenasnameh) of the parents and the child Proof of nationality of both parents Parents' birth certificates If applicable Parents' Marriage Certificate If previous marriages existed: divorce judgment or Death certificate of the previous spouse Proof of the mother’s marital status if the child was born outside the marriage If applicable Recognition of paternity If applicable Adoption decision If applicable Birth Certificates of Younger Siblings ATTENTION! Intergenerational (non-acquisition of German citizenship for children of German parents born abroad) Your child may not automatically acquire German citizenship. On the basis of the following questions, you can check whether your child has so-called Intergenerational is affected: Are you a German citizen or a German citizen? Were you born AFTER 31.12.1999? Were you not born in Germany, but abroad? Do you expect a child who will also not be born in Germany, but abroad? If you can answer all questions in the affirmative, please note the information in Intergenerational leaflet . Divorce Information on the recognition of a foreign divorce in Germany Please ask this Information leaflet . The application for recognition of a foreign divorce is submitted to you here available. All information on this page is based on findings and assessments of the message at the time of writing. However, no guarantee can be given for the completeness and correctness, in particular because of changes that have occurred in the meantime. title: Germany and Iran: Bilateral relations
text: Germany and Iran: Bilateral relations 05.05.2026 - Artikel Country name Islamic Republic of Iran, Jomhuri-ye Eslami-ye Iran (Farsi) Establishment of diplomatic relations: 26.02.1952 (Federal Republic of Germany) 07.12.1972 (German Democratic Republic) Form of government: Islamic Republic (since 1 April 1979) In 1952, diplomatic relations between the Federal Republic of Germany and Iran were established and the Iranian Embassy in Germany was opened. After the Islamic Revolution in 1979, relations between the two countries are sometimes very tense. The German Government is particularly concerned about the human rights situation in Iran, as is the Iran's nuclear programme and Iranian regional policy. Germany has in UN Human Rights Council in Geneva to establish an independent fact-finding mission to address human rights violations during the crackdown on protests in autumn 2022 and supports the mandate of the Special Rapporteur on Human Rights in Iran. Since the brutal repression of the latest protest movement in January 2026, Germany has imposed new sanctions on Iran and suspended the Iranian Revolutionary Guard (IRGC) terrorist listing. EU -level reached. In the framework of the Universal State Review Mechanism () UPR Germany recommended Iran Among others Stop executions, end discrimination against women and ethnic and religious minorities and guarantee freedom of expression, assembly and press. German-Iranian economic relations were close for a long time. However, the volume of trade has decreased greatly due to sanctions against Iran. In 2017, German-Iranian trade amounted to 3.4 billion euros and fell to 1.7 billion by 2019. EUR. In 2024, the foreign trade volume amounted to approximately 1.5 billion euros. This reduction can be mainly due to the withdrawal of USA Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action / JCPoA in 2018 and those of the USA unilaterally restore sanctions. After continued violations of the JCPoA by Iran since 2019, Germany, France and the United Kingdom in August 2025 UN Resolution 2231 provided for snapback mechanism. In accordance with the provisions of JCPoA 30 days after triggering the snapback, a total of six previously suspended UN Resolutions are back in force, i.e. at the end of September 2025. By re-enacting this resolution, for example, the transfer of nuclear or missile-related goods to all UN - member states banned from Iran; Also, an arms embargo against Iran was imposed. UN - Security Council imposed. Traditionally, there has been a lively exchange between Germany and Iran in the fields of culture and science, but this is currently heavily burdened and declining as a result of changing political conditions. Nevertheless, civil society interest in bilateral exchange in culture and science remains high. Info This text constitutes basic information. It shall be regularly updated. A guarantee for the accuracy and completeness of the information cannot be assumed. title: History of Law Firm and Residence
text: History of Law Firm and Residence Artikel Germany has had a diplomatic representation in Iran for more than 100 years. Already on 11 June 1873 a treaty of friendship and shipping was concluded between the German Reich and the then Persia, which also included the mutual establishment of permanent representations. The first German legation at the court of Nasreddin Shah was established eleven years later, on 20 October 1884, by the envoy on an extraordinary mission Ernst von Braunschweig. The current office property in the heart of Tehran was acquired ten years later by the envoy Count von Wallwitz for the German Reich. It is located next to the Turkish embassy, near the British, Russian and French embassies and the main Iranian government buildings. The first land register registration took place in 1931, but the two-storey building was already built in 1884-1886 by a European architect. After being acquired by the German Reich, it was rebuilt and renovated several times until it had to be demolished and rebuilt in 1937. In the middle of the Second World War, 1941, the new buildings of the office buildings were completed and immediately confiscated by the Imperial Iranian Government. The Swedish Embassy entered the office, as Sweden acted as the protective power of Germany during the war. In 1949, the property was handed over to the Americans, who used it until the completion of their own embassy in 1953. This year the confiscation was lifted. The ownership of the law firm was only finally secured by the Federal Republic of Germany in 1977 by rewriting. The residence of the German Ambassador in the district of Elahiye, in the quieter north of the city, was acquired by purchase contract in 1904. It was also confiscated in 1941 and subsequently used as a residence by the Swedish Embassy during the summer months. Two years later, the Iranian Foreign Ministry took over the building, which initially served as a summer residence for the Iranian Prime Minister and was later used for receptions. In 1953, his confiscation was also lifted. The rewriting in the land register took place, as in the law firm, but only in 1977. Until 1974, there was a Qajaren castle on the property, which finally had to be demolished due to its disrepair. The new building was completed in 1977. title: Human rights: the foundation of German foreign policy
text: Human rights: the foundation of German foreign policy 07.07.2026 - Artikel Germany is committed to human rights worldwide. The commitment to human rights is not only a fundamental value of our foreign policy – it also serves German interests. Universal Declaration of Human Rights Foundation of every human community Moral obligation and political interest The best defender of human rights: a vibrant civil society Germany as a State Party A logo for human rights The international symbol of human rights. © AA Universal Declaration of Human Rights It was the vision of a better world that brought together in 1948, over 70 years ago, men and women from all parts of the world in Paris. A world where all human beings are protected in their dignity as individuals: they adopted the Universal Declaration of Human Rights. It proceeds from the “innate dignity and equal and inalienable rights” of human beings and speaks these to every human being, without allowing any difference in skin color, gender, language, religion or other criteria. Human rights belong to every human being - solely on the basis of his humanity and completely independent of characteristics that can distinguish us humans from each other. Foundation of every human community Germany’s commitment to human rights is also a lesson from the darkest chapter of German history. Article 1 of the Basic Law therefore enshrines human dignity and inviolable and inalienable human rights as the basis of every human community, peace and justice in the world. The Basic Law therefore not only guarantees human rights in Germany, but obliges us to work all over the world for the protection of human dignity and fundamental freedoms. Moral obligation and political interest Lars Castellucci, Federal Government Commissioner for Human Rights Policy and Humanitarian Aid © DBT/Stella von Saldern But advocating for human rights is not only a moral and international obligation under the Basic Law. Respect for human rights is also in Germany’s foreign policy interest. Examples around the world show that where human rights are violated, there can be no peace and no stable development in the long term. Protected freedom and human dignity, however, have proven to be the engine of creativity and prosperity. The Federal Foreign Office is working alongside human rights policy. Federal Government Commissioner for Human Rights Policy and Humanitarian Aid , Lars Castellucci , also a specialised unit for human rights. Together they use many different instruments: funding programmes for non-governmental organisations, political dialogues, public statements or silent diplomacy have already improved the human rights situation in many countries. Info Information on German human rights policy in easy language can be found here Reading. The best defender of human rights: a vibrant civil society Particularly important for the protection of human rights is a public that looks at possible violations of rights. Germany’s commitment is a particular focus here. In dialogue with NGOs, religious groups, intellectuals and committed personalities, Germany promotes vibrant and attentive civil societies worldwide. Because if citizens can engage in groups and public forums independent of the state, this is one of the best assurances against the violation of human rights. More on Civil Society and Human Rights Article 1 of the Basic Law: Human dignity is inviolable. © photothek.net Germany as a State Party On the basis of UN The Charter and the Universal Declaration of Human Rights have established a comprehensive treaty system for the protection of human rights. Germany is a party to all major UN human rights conventions and their additional protocols and regularly reports to the treaty committees on the implementation of the respective treaty obligations. In Europe too, there is a dense network of institutions for the protection of human rights. In addition to the European Union, the key roles are played by the Council of Europe and the OSCE : The protection of fundamental rights in EU Council of Europe Organisation for Security and Cooperation in Europe A logo for human rights The Universal Logo for Human Rights © AA The human rights logo can here downloaded for free. His design emerged in 2011 from an international competition initiated by Germany. From over 15,000 entries from 190 countries, a global international jury selected Among others Several human rights activists and Nobel Peace Prize winners such as Shirin Ebadi and Mikhail Gorbachev belonged. The symbol of a bird and a hand of Predrag Stakić from Serbia prevailed and has since been used by the Federal Government and internationally as a symbol of human rights.
